metadata.source: Plant detail completion pass for reserved batches plants-a and plants-b
notes: Adds species-specific plant detail fields including food_quality and commonness.
Leaves links and photo credits for follow-up review where not yet assigned.

plant_parts: Fruit: late summer to early fall; edible ripe red berries; mild flavor and soft/mealy texture.
Leaves and stems are not a food item.
Warnings: safe only when fully identified; fruit is edible but not a high-value berry.
records.culinary_uses: Ripe berries can be eaten fresh in small handfuls or added to mixed berry sauces and trail snacks, but they are usually considered more interesting than delicious.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food and traditional-use plant rather than a major modern medicinal. Some Indigenous uses are recorded for related dogwood-group plants, but this is not a primary self-treatment species.
records.notes: Low woodland groundcover of cool moist conifer and mixed forest. Four white bracts surround the flower head, followed by a tight cluster of red berries. A useful northern indicator plant. Harvest lightly because patches spread slowly in some sites.
look_alikes: Other low whorled woodland plants before fruiting

plant_parts: Young shoots: spring; peel and cook like asparagus.
Young leaves: spring to early summer; best as a cooked green or tea plant.
Flowers: summer; edible as garnish.
Warnings: older leaves and stems get tough and stringy.
records.culinary_uses: Young shoots are the best food use and can be steamed, sautéed, or added to mixed spring vegetable dishes. Leaves can be used fresh or dried for tea. Flowers are mainly garnish.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditionally used as a soothing tea herb for mild digestive irritation and as a gentle anti-inflammatory plant. Some modern herbal interest focuses on tannins and anti-inflammatory compounds, especially in teas and extracts.
records.notes: Tall colonizing plant of burns, logging cuts, roadsides, and sunny openings. One of the most recognizable northern edible greens. Good beginner plant if harvested young. It is also useful for tinder and fiber historically.

plant_parts: Flowers: summer; edible in small amounts as garnish.
Seeds: late summer to fall; edible but tiny and fussy to gather.
Warnings: this is mainly a medicinal/topical-interest plant, not a bulk food plant.
records.culinary_uses: Flowers and small seeds are edible, but this is more of a nibble-or-garnish species than a serious foraging food.
records.medicinal_uses: Best known for traditional topical use on itchy rashes and irritated skin, especially in folk use around nettle or mild plant-contact irritation. The fresh watery sap is the main reason people remember it.
records.notes: Succulent wet-soil annual with orange spotted flowers and explosive seed pods. Usually found in damp shade, stream edges, and rich disturbed wet ground. Useful to know, but not a major calorie or flavor plant.

plant_parts: Fruit: late summer through fall, often best after full ripening or light frost; pulp is the main edible part.
Warnings: large flat seeds are discarded.
records.culinary_uses: Ripe fruits can be eaten fresh, mashed into leather-like fruit paste, or cooked into sauces and preserves. Good enough to justify gathering when a tree fruits well.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional use is secondary to its food value. Some viburnums were used in folk medicine, but nannyberry is mainly worth knowing as an edible fruit.
records.notes: Shrub or small tree with opposite leaves and blue-black hanging fruit clusters. The fully ripe fruit has a sweet, dark, almost prune-like quality. Not everywhere, but a worthwhile native fruit when found.

plant_parts: Leaves: spring through fall; used fresh or dried for tea and seasoning.
Warnings: strongly aromatic, so it is used in small quantities rather than as a vegetable.
records.culinary_uses: Leaves can be dried for tea or used to scent broths, meats, and campfire cooking. Best treated as a northern aromatic spice plant.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional tea herb used for digestive complaints and as a mild astringent. Aromatic compounds are the main reason for its herbal value.
records.notes: Not a fern at all, but a low aromatic shrub of sandy openings, barrens, and pine country. Narrow lobed leaves smell distinctive when crushed. Easy to learn and worth knowing for tea.

plant_parts: Leaves: spring to summer; best in small amounts as seasoning or tea.
Flowers: summer; tea and topical herb use.
Warnings: bitterness limits food use; some people react to Aster family plants.
records.culinary_uses: Best used sparingly as seasoning or tea. It is not a bulk edible, but it has real value as a camp herb or bitter aromatic accent.
records.medicinal_uses: Classic wound and topical herb with traditional use for minor bleeding, fever support, and digestive bitters. Its medicinal interest is much stronger than its food value.
records.notes: Feathery leaves, flat-topped white flower clusters, and a strong crushed aroma. Easy to recognize in fields and roadsides. A very useful medicinal-knowledge plant, but not a high-quality edible.
look_alikes: Poison hemlock seedlings at a careless glance
Other white-flowered Aster-family plants before bloom

plant_parts: Root: first-year plants in fall through spring; cooked vegetable.
Young leaves: spring; cooked green, often somewhat bitter.
Flower buds and flowers: summer; edible in small amounts.
Seeds: oil-bearing but small.
Warnings: second-year roots become woody.
records.culinary_uses: First-year roots are the main food part and can be cooked like a small root vegetable. Young leaves and buds are edible but secondary. This is a real edible plant, not just a garnish species.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditionally used for soothing and anti-inflammatory purposes. Modern interest often centers on seed oil rich in gamma-linolenic acid, though that is more supplement territory than field use.
records.notes: Biennial with a basal rosette first year and tall yellow-flowered stalk second year. Likes disturbed ground and open sunny places. Worth learning because different parts are useful at different stages.
look_alikes: Other rosette-forming roadside plants before flowering

plant_parts: Berry-like cones: second year when blue and mature; spice and tea use.
Warnings: use only true junipers; avoid confusing with yew. Strong flavor means small amounts. Avoid heavy medicinal use in kidney disease or pregnancy contexts.
records.culinary_uses: Ripe blue cones are used in very small amounts to flavor game, sauerkraut, pickles, and teas. This is a seasoning plant, not a fruit you eat by the bowl.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional diuretic and warming aromatic herb. The resinous terpenes are the main reason for both flavor and medicinal action.
records.notes: Low shrub or small tree depending on species and site. Needle-like leaves are sharp. In practical foraging, the edible value is mostly as a spice and tea plant. Only a few cones are needed.

plant_parts: Petals: spring to early summer; fresh garnish, jelly, syrup.
Hips: fall after ripening; tea, jelly, puree, syrup.
Warnings: remove irritating hairs and seeds from hips before heavy use.
records.culinary_uses: Petals can be used in desserts, syrups, and teas. Rose hips are the more important food part and can be turned into tea, jelly, syrup, and puree.
records.medicinal_uses: Rose hips are traditional vitamin-C-rich winter tea material. Petals and hips also appear in gentle soothing preparations and aromatic blends.
records.notes: Prickly shrub with compound leaves and pink to white flowers. One of the more useful dual-purpose plants because both petals and hips matter. Gather lightly so wildlife retains winter fruit.

plant_parts: Leaves: spring; best young, raw or lightly cooked.
Rhizomes/tubers: spring; pungent condiment use in very small amounts.
Warnings: small plant; harvest lightly because patches can be easy to overpick.
records.culinary_uses: A spring mustard with peppery leaves and small underground parts that can be used like a wild cress or pungent garnish. More of a spring flavor plant than a staple food.
records.medicinal_uses: Minor traditional mustard-family warming use, but mostly valued as a spring edible.
records.notes: Woodland spring ephemeral with divided leaves and white to pale pink flowers. Useful because it appears before the forest fully closes in. Best for nibbling or salad accents, not bulk harvest.

plant_parts: Young shoots: spring; cooked after proper preparation.
Flower buds: summer; cooked vegetable.
Young pods: early summer when small and tender; cooked.
Flowers: summer; fritters and garnish.
Warnings: correct species matters; not all milkweeds are handled the same; latex sap and dogbane confusion are serious issues.
records.culinary_uses: A real wild vegetable when correctly identified and gathered at the right stage. Shoots, buds, flowers, and very young pods are the useful parts, typically cooked rather than eaten casually raw.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional medicinal references exist, but this is a caution-heavy plant and better treated first as an edible requiring skill, not as a casual medicine.
records.notes: Tall colony-forming plant with opposite leaves, thick stems, white latex, and pink flower balls. Very useful, but not a beginner plant unless the ID and preparation are rock solid. Harvest lightly because it is also an important monarch host.

plant_parts: Very young peeled shoots and leaf stalks: spring to early summer; cooked use only.
Warnings: sap can cause photodermatitis on sun-exposed skin; giant hogweed confusion is unacceptable; this is a skilled-use plant.
records.culinary_uses: Traditionally used only when very young, peeled, and cooked. Not a casual trail nibble. Mostly worthwhile to experienced plant foragers who know the carrot family danger zone well.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional use exists, but strong caution belongs ahead of amateur medicinal use because handling mistakes matter more than theory.
records.notes: Large white-umbel plant of moist edges and openings. Useful only with confidence, gloves, and full awareness of look-alikes and sap issues. Better respected than romanticized.

plant_parts: Leaves: year-round, best gathered sparingly for tea.
Warnings: use moderately; this is not an everyday beverage plant in large doses.
records.culinary_uses: Mainly a tea herb with a distinctive resinous northern flavor. Not a general edible.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional tea plant used for colds, coughs, and as a warming aromatic herb. The aromatic leaf chemistry is the main point.
records.notes: Bog and muskeg shrub with narrow leathery leaves, rusty undersides, and clusters of white flowers. A classic northern tea plant. Good to know, but moderation belongs in the notes.
look_alikes: Bog rosemary and other bog shrubs before close inspection

plant_parts: Rhizome: spring through fall; small flavoring or tea use after correct identification.
Warnings: use only true sweet flag and not yellow-flag iris or cattail lookalikes; strong flavor means small amounts.
records.culinary_uses: Rhizome has been candied, dried, chewed, or used in tiny amounts for tea and flavoring. More of an aromatic specialty plant than a bulk edible.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional digestive and warming herb. Its aromatic oils are the reason for both flavor and medicinal use.
records.notes: Wetland iris-like plant with sword leaves and a fragrant rhizome. Not the easiest beginner edible, but a classic old-time aromatic if identified correctly.

plant_parts: Inner bark and twigs: medicinal interest rather than food use.
Warnings: not a casual food plant; salicylate caution matters, especially with aspirin sensitivity, anticoagulants, ulcers, or kidney concerns.
records.culinary_uses: Not a real food plant in normal foraging practice. At most it belongs in survival or medicinal discussion, not dinner planning.
records.medicinal_uses: Classic source of salicylate-related pain and fever support in traditional medicine. The historical chemistry matters here more than the culinary angle.
records.notes: Common shrub and tree group of wetlands, shorelines, and open ground. Important medicinal-history species but should be framed with contraindication caution, not as a romantic cure-all.
look_alikes: Other narrow-leaved wetland shrubs before close ID

plant_parts: Fruit: mid to late summer depending on species and site.
Leaves: medicinal-history use exists but not a normal food part.
Warnings: leave some fruit for wildlife and avoid stripping sparse patches.
records.culinary_uses: Excellent fresh eating berry and one of the best wild fruits for pies, pancakes, jam, syrup, freezing, and dehydration.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional leaf use exists for astringent and blood-sugar-oriented folk use, but the fruit is the main practical value.
records.notes: A top-tier northern forage fruit. Multiple species occur in Upper Michigan, from lowbush to wetland and highbush types. Worth planning trips around in season.

plant_parts: Fruit: mid to late summer.
Young leaves: tea use possible.
Warnings: berries are fragile and do not keep well, so they are best eaten fresh or processed quickly.
records.culinary_uses: Excellent fresh trail berry and very good for jam, sauce, and quick desserts, though too soft for much storage or transport.
records.medicinal_uses: Leaves have traditional astringent tea use similar to other Rubus species.
records.notes: Soft red berry on thornless canes with big maple-like leaves. One of the easiest pleasant trail berries in the U.P. because the plant is distinctive and the fruit is obvious when ripe.

plant_parts: Fruit: early to midsummer depending on species and site.
Warnings: birds strip them fast, so timing matters.
records.culinary_uses: One of the best wild fruits in the region for fresh eating, pie, syrup, jam, drying, and fruit leather. High-value forage.
records.medicinal_uses: Primarily a food fruit. Traditional use exists but is secondary to the berry value.
records.notes: Small tree or shrub with white spring bloom and purple-red ripe berries. A serious wild fruit, not just a nibble plant. Worth checking repeatedly when near ripe because birds beat people to it.
look_alikes: Chokecherry before fruit color and leaf details are checked

plant_parts: Fruit: early to midsummer.
Leaves: tea use possible.
Warnings: fruit is tiny, so gathering is slower than cultivated berries.
records.culinary_uses: Excellent fresh berry with concentrated flavor. Best eaten out of hand, on cereal, or used in small-batch jam and desserts.
records.medicinal_uses: Leaves have mild traditional astringent tea use.
records.notes: Tiny but superb flavor. One of the best examples of a wild plant where quantity is the only real drawback. Easy to learn from trifoliate leaves, white flowers, and runners.

plant_parts: Fruit: summer; ripe berries best for fresh eating, underripe better for cooking.
Warnings: thorns on some species and variable fruit quality depending on species and ripeness.
records.culinary_uses: Very good for pies, sauces, jam, and fresh eating when fully ripe. Often more useful in the kitchen than on the trail because tartness can be strong before full ripeness.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a fruit plant rather than an important medicinal species.
records.notes: Shrubby Ribes with translucent or striped berries depending on species. Not always abundant, but worthwhile when found. A good preserving fruit.
look_alikes: Currants before checking stem prickles and berry clusters

plant_parts: Flowers: early summer for fritters, cordial, and tea.
Berries: late summer to fall, cooked use preferred.
Warnings: raw berries and especially stems, leaves, and unripe fruit can cause stomach upset.
records.culinary_uses: Excellent cooked fruit for syrup, jelly, wine, and medicinal-style preparations. Flowers are also useful. This is a real harvest plant when processed properly.
records.medicinal_uses: Classic cold-season folk plant, especially in syrup and tea form. Anthocyanin-rich berries are the main reason for its popularity.
records.notes: Umbels of cream flowers followed by dark berry clusters. Strong value, but needs processing discipline. Do not glamorize raw use.
look_alikes: Red-berried baneberry at a careless glance before structure is checked

plant_parts: Fruit: fall, often better after frost or after cooking.
Warnings: flavor is variable and some people dislike the musky note; correct American type matters if separating from ornamental viburnums.
records.culinary_uses: Usually better cooked than raw, especially for jelly, sauce, and mixed-fruit preserves. More useful than delicious for many people.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional use exists, but the fruit is the main foraging angle here.
records.notes: Opposite-leaved shrub with translucent red berry clusters. A legitimate native food, though not everybody loves the flavor. Worth knowing because it is common and productive.

plant_parts: Fruit: summer.
Leaves: tea and flavoring history exists.
Warnings: strong flavor divides people; some sites may have limited local abundance.
records.culinary_uses: Very good for jam, syrup, cordial, baking, and mixed berry preserves. Usually more of a kitchen berry than an eat-by-the-handful trail fruit.
records.medicinal_uses: Berry and leaf use both appear in traditional practice, mainly for antioxidant-rich fruit and mild herbal teas.
records.notes: Strong-smelling currant fruit with serious preserving value. Good species to include because flavor and use are distinct from red currants and gooseberries.

plant_parts: Fruit: summer.
Warnings: usually best when fully ripe or cooked; tartness is the main character.
records.culinary_uses: Excellent jelly, sauce, syrup, and baking berry. Can be eaten fresh, but is usually more useful cooked or sweetened.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food fruit with some light traditional astringent or vitamin-oriented use.
records.notes: Translucent red currants in hanging clusters. A good preserving berry and easier to appreciate in the kitchen than raw in large quantity.

plant_parts: Fruit: late summer to fall, usually after full ripening.
Warnings: raw fruit can be quite drying and astringent, so it shines better processed.
records.culinary_uses: Excellent for juice, wine, syrup, jam, leather, and mixed fruit products. Better as an ingredient berry than a casual fresh-snacking fruit.
records.medicinal_uses: Anthocyanin-rich fruit with strong antioxidant reputation. Mostly a food-with-health-angle species.
records.notes: Dark berry clusters on a shrub with good fall color. Worth including because it is productive and useful even if fresh-eating quality is not top tier.

plant_parts: Fruit: late summer; best cooked, dried, or processed.
Warnings: pits are not eaten; leaves and wilted browse issues matter more for livestock than fruit use. Raw fruit is often puckery.
records.culinary_uses: Very good for jelly, syrup, juice, and traditional dried preparations. Better processed than eaten fresh in bulk.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional use exists, but this is mainly a preserving fruit.
records.notes: Long dangling racemes of dark fruit on a shrub or small tree. Common enough to matter. A practical wild fruit when you treat it as a processing berry.
look_alikes: Pin cherry before checking fruit clusters

plant_parts: Fruit: fall; pulp is the main edible part.
Flowers and leaves: traditional medicinal use.
Warnings: seeds are discarded; variable species and heavy thorns slow gathering.
records.culinary_uses: Can be eaten fresh when ripe or used in jelly, ketchup-style sauces, syrup, and leather, but texture is often mealy. More useful than exciting.
records.medicinal_uses: Important traditional heart-support herb, especially fruit, leaf, and flower. Flavonoids and related compounds are the reason it gets serious herbal attention.
records.notes: Thorny shrub or small tree with clustered pomes. Good medicinal-history species and a usable fruit, but rarely a top recreational eating berry.

plant_parts: Berries: late summer through winter.
Leaves: tea use.
Warnings: strong methyl-salicylate chemistry means this is a flavor plant, not a bulk food.
records.culinary_uses: Berries can be eaten fresh in modest amounts and leaves can be made into tea. More of a flavor and trail-snack plant than a major food source.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional tea and flavor-medicine plant used for mild aches and colds. The wintergreen chemistry is the whole story here.
records.notes: Low evergreen woodland plant with red berries and unmistakable aroma. A good morale-boosting trail nibble, though not a bucket-filling fruit.
look_alikes: Partridgeberry before checking leaf shape and aroma

plant_parts: Fresh spring tips: spring; tea, syrup, seasoning.
Warnings: use true hemlock tree only; do not confuse with poison hemlock, the unrelated herb.
records.culinary_uses: Fresh tips make bright tea, syrup, jelly, candy, and infused sugar. A very good conifer flavoring plant when gathered lightly.
records.medicinal_uses: Tea has traditional cold-season use and vitamin-C reputation. Aromatic compounds and fresh green growth are the main appeal.
records.notes: Soft flat-needled conifer of cool woods and ravines. One of the best conifer tip flavors. Harvest lightly from many branches rather than stripping a few twigs bare.
look_alikes: Fir and spruce tips if gathered carelessly

plant_parts: Fresh new tips: spring; tea, syrup, jelly, vinegar, seasoning.
Warnings: harvest only the soft bright new growth and do not overstrip young trees.
records.culinary_uses: Excellent spring flavoring plant for tea, syrup, jelly, candy, vinegar, beer, and seasoning. One of the best northern tree foods in the right season.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional conifer tea for colds and general winter-spring tonic use. The fresh tips are valued for aroma and vitamin-C reputation.
records.notes: A highly practical northern seasonal food. Easy to use and often abundant. Flavor varies by species, but the category is solid. Great example of a wild plant with both taste and utility.
look_alikes: Fir and hemlock tips if species-specific flavor matters
